acronyms.usage: The first practical public-key cryptosystem named after its inventors Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman in 1977

acronyms.expansion: Euler's Totient Function - counts integers coprime to n
acronyms.usage: For product of primes p×q, φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1)
Method to combine congruences modulo p and q to compute modular exponentiation faster
Provably secure padding scheme for RSA encryption
Padding scheme for RSA signatures providing provable security

analogies.analogy: Like a mailbox with two keys: anyone can drop letters (encrypt with public key), but only the owner with the private key can retrieve them (decrypt). The special lock (n) is public, but the key to open it (d) is secret.

The primes p and q are like the two halves of a broken plate. Anyone can see the public plate (n), but reconstructing the exact original broken pieces (factoring) to find the private key is computationally hard.

OAEP padding is like putting your letter in a sealed, numbered envelope with a random code before putting it in the mailbox - even if someone knows the message format, they can't predict or manipulate the padded content.

mnemonics.content: P-E-N: Primes (p,q) → Euler's Totient (φ) → iNverse (d) - Step-by-step RSA key generation
mnemonics.title: RSA Key Generation Steps

mnemonics.content: E N C: Encryption uses Public (e, n), Decryption uses Private (d, n), Ciphertext results (C)
mnemonics.title: RSA Encryption/Decryption Formula

mnemonics.content: pErform qUickly uNtil tRying Harder: p×q=n; e×d≡1(mod φ(n)); RSA relies on factoring being hard.
mnemonics.title: RSA Core Equation

mnemonics.content: CrYptogrAPhy NeedS Secure Keys: C = M^e mod n; M = C^d mod n; RSA = Rivest Shamir Adleman
mnemonics.title: RSA Formulas

visualTips.visualization: Flowchart: [Select primes p,q] → [Compute n=p×q, φ(n)=(p-1)(q-1)] → [Choose e with gcd(e,φ)=1] → [Compute d=e⁻¹ mod φ(n)] → [Public: (e,n), Private: (d,n)]
Two parallel tubes: Sender has M → encrypts with (e,n) → produces C (modular exponentiation) → transmits C → Receiver decrypts with (d,n) → recovers M = C^d mod n
Split the large exponentiation into two smaller ones: compute M_p = C^d mod p (fast, small numbers), compute M_q = C^d mod q, then merge using: M = M_q + q × ((M_p - M_q) × q⁻¹ mod p)
